speaker: interviewer
text: --8th, 1995. Could you describe for us a typical day living under German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I mentioned, my parents took in three or four families whose houses were burned by the Germans the day they occupied our hometown, so the house was terribly crowded. Usually it was a family to a room. There was very little space. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People couldn't go to work in most instances, although the Germans did allow the tanneries to run for a while. And they allowed my father and my brothers to work in the tannery. But we couldn't sell our products. They liquidated everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: As for children, there was no school at all. Next door to us, our neighbors also took in several families, including a shoemaker. And of course, people always need shoes, so he set up a little bench in the house next door to us to make shoes for the Germans, for the Poles. And my parents said, gee, why don't you help him out? So I went in. Every day I would go next door and actually be an apprentice to a shoemaker. And I actually learned how to make shoes. That would occupy most of my time.

speaker: interviewee
text: As for the women, it was a terrible struggle to find food. Starvation was throughout. You couldn't buy anything. They didn't really let you grow very much. We did have a garden. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I should say that we always had to depend on our chickens and our own cows for milk and eggs and so on. But during the German occupation, quite often, our Polish neighbors would seize and return most of it over to the-- to them or to the German commandant, so that there was practically no food. You couldn't buy very much. And the women just spent all of their time trying to somehow make meals out of next to nothing and to keep clean, because with so many people in one house, it was very easy to spread diseases and epidemics and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all in all-- and of course, the young men, the minute you would hear the German-- a German truck drove into town, you'd start running to hide. Nights were long. Rumors would fly. Hope was practically nonexistent. And people just tried to make the best of the situation, but it was not a very pleasant way of spending a day, day by day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, at least once a week there were actual killings, and you had to bury the dead. Quite often, you had to go at night and dig them out from graves where the Germans dropped them and bring them to the cemetery. So it was-- life was actually preoccupied with death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to observe Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We continued to observe, but all of it was turned upside down. You couldn't really go to synagogues. They actually closed the synagogues. And so, yes, you could pray in private. You could celebrate in private. You couldn't, for instance, bake matzoh for the holidays or openly celebrate anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your family observed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we observed as best we could. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how did the events of late January change things?

speaker: interviewee
text: The events of late January [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] changed terribly. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] 1943 was a very cold winter. The temperature had dropped to way below zero, and we had lots of snow. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the middle of the night, we heard trucks. And then we heard trucks coming through the streets, including [POLISH] where we lived, and loudspeakers announcing that all Jews must get dressed, must meet in the center of town near the church once again. Anybody found not in the center of town after 2 hours would be shot on the spot. And so we knew that liquidation had come. And we had very little time to get dressed or to prepare. We immediately decided to run into our hiding place above the tannery to see if we could hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of day was this that you heard the trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably about 4 o'clock in the morning. It was a very cold, wintery long night. Some of my brothers and sisters could not possibly hide, because they had little children. My brother Meyer had a child. My sister Mariashe had two children. And my sister Dverke had a little child-- a little boy. They could not hide, because the children would give us away. So they had no choice, with many others, to march off into town. My sister Dverke, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was very emotional, she grabbed her child, her baby, and ran into the garden where the well was. And she jumped into the well with her child in her arms. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Those of us who could, including my father, my mother, my brothers, my brother Maishe, my other sisters, and some of the other people who were not members of our family but who lived in our house, we all ended up crawling up to the crawlspace to the hiding place above the tannery. I don't know if perhaps this might be good idea to show you or to describe how this hiding place worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The hiding place we built above the tannery on the second story-- the whole second story was a triangle. My father had built a drying room for drying leather on that second story, so that-- so that there was a crawlspace above it. The crawlspace was about 20 feet wide, and about-- at the highest point, about 3 and 1/2, 4 feet high. And the building was a very long building, so that we constructed a little opening [POINTS] at the other end, at the back end of the tannery, where a ladder-- a rope ladder-- could be dropped. You climbed up on a rope ladder until you were in the crawlspace. Then you crawled on your hands and knees about 80 or 90 feet to the front of the tannery, facing towards the street, where we had built a blind wall with boards that lifted out-- a whole little gate that lifted out-- where you could crawl in, put that little gate back into place, and that would create a wall just like the original wall, so that even if somebody came looking for us and crawled in that crawlspace, they would come to a wall that they thought was the end of the tannery, not knowing that behind that wall was about a 15- or 20-foot extra space where human beings were holding their breath, hiding. And there were also cracks in those boards so that we could see what's going on on the street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as many of us as could ran to that hiding place. We did not have time to dress, so we grabbed whatever we could, blankets, sheets, quilts, and a little bit of food. And we ran into that hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say probably about 16, 17 people. We could see through the cracks what was going on in town-- the crying, the driving of old men and women. We heard the shots. People were killed right in the street. And everybody was driven out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was with you in this space of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of my family-- my father, my mother, my sister Tzipe, my-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my sister Feitche, my sister Baile, my niece Roske, and then many non-family members. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Suddenly, after a while, things became quiet. Everyone who left for the center of town had left. Those who didn't leave, the Germans and the Poles found and shot on the spot, mostly. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this from your hiding space?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could see some of them walking in the street, yes. And-- and we could-- we could see that and hear that very-- very clearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recognize people that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. We could see them quite well. After a while, it was quiet, except that a neighbor of ours-- the same Bolek whose daughter went to get medicine when my father was beaten-- and this Bolek actually worked for my father in the tannery, and we always treated him well. And we thought he was a good neighbor. But the minute they liquidated town, he became frantic. He wanted our house. And he knew that we did not march off to town, so he knew that we were hiding, but he didn't know where. And he came around with a rifle looking for us. And then he brought the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, saying, we know that they are there, because they didn't march off. Only so-and-so and so-and-so marched off. The rest of them didn't, so we know that they are here. We've got to find them. We've got to find them. We've got to kill them. And he came with-- he had a rifle himself. In the tannery, there was a huge boiler where steam would be produced for the process of cleaning hides. And he thought maybe we were hiding in that steamer, so he shot bullets through the steamer, hoping to kill us if we are there, or at least find us. And we could hear the shots. We were right above in that hiding place. And he and his family were going crazy trying to find us. Well, it was very cold. We couldn't stay there. Every now and then, we heard shots, the next day even, so we knew that the Germans were still surrounding the town and watching. But we had no choice. It was either starve and freeze to death or get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long all together were you in the hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days and two nights. The second night, we decided we would make a break for it. It was decided that my father, who was the oldest, and I, the youngest, would make a  break first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was your father at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father at this time was probably close to 60, I would say-- maybe in his 60s. And he was only half-alive, blind in one eye after the beating of a year and a half ago. And then my sister Tzipe and my mother would be the next two to follow. And the agreement was that we would reach the woods. We would wait for them in the woods until they came. And then others would come from our family. And then we would all go to the farmer who promised to give us shelter. My father and I covered ourselves with sheets, white sheets. And we crawled on our hands and knees for maybe, I don't know, 5,000 feet. And then we just ran. And we made it to the woods. At the edge of the woods, we were waiting for my mother and sister. Unfortunately, they were spotted by the  Germans and shot. And we saw that. At that point, we knew that none of the others would be able to come out there and then. And so my father and I decided to go while it's still nighttime to the farmer who promised us shelter. We got there and knocked on the door. And he came to the door, and he wouldn't even let us in the house. He wouldn't even give us a piece of bread. He said, either you go, or I call the Germans. So he betrayed us. He had all of our things and would not even give us a piece of bread. At that point we were half-frozen, both of us, and starving. We had no place to go, so we walked back into the woods. And my father froze in my arms, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at which point I was also pretty badly frozen. I left my father there, covered him with snow, left him there, and decided I would go back to town so that the Germans could shoot me. It's easier to be shot than to freeze to death. I was on my way back to town. I was passing a farmhouse when a Polish woman, Czarnecka-- I gave you her name-- she-- it was a Sunday morning, and she-- and her family had gone to church. She saw me walking. She came and asked me where I was going. She said she knew my family. And when I told her I was going to town, what had happened to my father, she said to me, look. My husband is an anti-Semite. And if he finds out that I am hiding you, he'll kill me, and he'll kill you. But I'm not going to let you die. I'm going to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hide you in-- in a barn where there were pigs on the main floor and straw and hay above. I'm going to hide you there. And when I can, when my husband is not home, and the children are not around, I'll bring you some medicine, some fat for your frozen body, and some food whenever I can. And she said to me, promise me that [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] every day you'll say, I'll die tomorrow, but not today. [CRYING] Well, she-- she kept her promise. She gave me some food and whatever she could. And a few weeks later, one night I heard knocking on the door. And two young men came begging for food. They were two brothers from my hometown, the older one, Chonen, and the younger one Ruvak. And they-- Ruvak was actually my brother's classmate, and Chonen was one year older than I. And they came begging for food. And the woman told them that she was hiding me in the barn. And that night, I joined up with them. And they were hiding out in the forest. It was very difficult to hide in the forest, because there was lots of snow, and it would leave trails. So they were hiding in farmers' barns. They would break a board off and crawl in and hide in the hay during the day and sometimes at night. And they would go out begging for food. And so I joined them. By that time, it must have been about March of 1943. And the weather improved a bit. And I joined Ruvak and Chonen. And we survived. There was one other, a fourth one. And the four of us were hiding out in barns with farmers in the forest until we were liberated-- early 1945. In order to survive in the forest-- the forests were not so large, and farmers would come through them, and Germans would come through them. And we knew that if we were to survive, we would have to have hiding places. So we actually built a hole in the ground, a bunker in the forest. And there again, I don't have a drawing of that yet, because it's part of the story where I haven't reached yet in my memoirs, but I will. And we built with nothing more than an ax, a shovel, and a couple of empty bags from potatoes-- you know, those burlap bags. We managed to build a beautiful bunker in the forest. We did it twice actually. What we would do is we would find an area in the forest where there were lots of large trees, but also a few small ones, young trees, not too heavy to lift out. We would dig out one of those little trees with a cone going this way, like a pumpkin, when you carve a pumpkin, the top, so that it would not fall in. That would hold the roots of the tree and a ball of earth. We would lift that out gently, put it aside. Then, we dug a hole for the four of us about six feet long, about four feet deep, and about four feet wide, or six feet wide maybe. And we found pieces of pipe that we put into the corners. We also dug around a larger area around this six by four hole. And then we went far away in the forest and we chopped down straight woods and made logs. We laid those out as a ceiling, about two feet below ground, then except for the opening, where, the tree was going to fit in as a door. Then we covered it with earth, about two feet of earth. Then we covered it with the leaves. And the earth that we dug out we carried on our shoulders, bag by bag, far away. We spread it in a different part of the forest, so that people wouldn't know where it came from. And we cleaned it all up so that you could have an army march over the bunker and they wouldn't know that human beings were underneath there. See, we would come in and then slowly lift that little tree over our head and put it in place. And when we wanted to get out, we would lift that little tree up, put it aside, and climb out. And that's how we were hiding for a good part of that period in the forest. Then one day we were discovered. We had to build another one. Life was just horrible during this period. We did not have facilities for taking a shower or bathing in a stream or anything for practically two years. During the summer we could find occasionally a stream where we could wash up, therefore we were full of lice. We had no food, practically. We were starving. We ate whatever we could catch in the forest. And occasionally, we would go out and beg for food. I lost most of my teeth during that period. They just rotted out. How we didn't get typhus or whatever, I don't know. But without hygiene, without food, it was just a nightmare. Now, to go out to beg for food was very dangerous. We had one interesting incident one summery night. It was late summer. The corn-- well, there in Poland, wheat they called corn. And so the wheat was fairly high. It was already brownish-yellow, almost ready to be cut, and very high. And we went out. We would take turns going out begging for food. And that night, Ruvak, the older of the two brothers, and I went off maybe five miles away to a village to go from door to door to ask if they would give us some bread, some bacon, whatever. And we had collected some food. It was a very bright night. There were-- there was a full moon. And we were walking on a path not far from the village, when suddenly from nowhere a group of Polish young men armed with pistols and rifles showed up and faced us and asked us to identify ourselves. And of course, they were out to look for Jews and kill them off.  They were helping the Germans. And we-- the minute we realized who they were, we didn't answer, and we dropped to the ground. We were on a narrow path and there was wheat on both sides. So I dropped on one side in the wheat and Ruvak dropped on the other side, and we started crawling on our hands and knees. And they were firing away from rifles and pistols at us. And I crawled as far as I could. And then I got up, and I ran. And I ran the whole five miles and came back into the forest. By the way, we developed a tremendous sense of directions. In the middle of the darkest night, I could find my way in the forest to our bunker. I don't know how, but somehow we developed a sixth sense. And I ran, and I ran maybe five, six miles without stopping. And when I got near the bunker I started hollering to Chonen and the other guy, Ruvak is dead, because I assumed that he got shot, because when I-- when the firing stopped, and everything was quiet, I called out, Ruvak, Ruvak, and he didn't answer, so I assumed that he was dead. And I hollered out, Ruvak is dead. And I told the other two what had happened. And we decided to wait about an hour and then to go look for his body. And about half an hour later, Ruvak came running. And before he reached the bunker, he hollered, Berl is dead. They used to call me Berl. He thought I was dead, so he was hollering that I was dead. And I thought he was dead. And so that-- that was how we had to gather food if we were to-- to survive day by day. It was nothing but filth and hunger, starvation and danger. There was hardly anybody who was willing to give us shelter. I should say that during this period, there were two families-- the one I've already mentioned who stopped me from going to Jasionowka to be shot-- and then another one, a Mrs. Dekar, who eventually gave us shelter in a barn with Ruvak and the others. And again, we dug a hole in the barn, similar construction, and we were hiding in her barn and going out and begging for food. But they took their own lives in their hands [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] giving us shelter. It was a very dangerous thing to do. If the other Poles or the Germans found out that a Polish farmer was helping a Jewish family, they were bound to come and burn the house and kill them. And they've done it many, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in this second farmer's barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were there on and off. We would go back to the forest when we could. And we would come back to Dekar's. And these went back and forth until such time, really, as we were liberated by the Russian army, early spring of 1945.
